meta.title: EPA 608 Trainer
meta.description: EPA 608 learning engine with 25 questions per section, study mode, exam mode, adaptive missed-question review, and section strength indicators.
meta.contentNote: Question wording is rewritten for learning use from EPA 608 concepts and study-guide topics.

Core.question: When transporting cylinders containing used refrigerant, DOT requires that you:
Core.explanation: Transport questions test full compliance. If all listed safety/legal steps are true, choose all of the above.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: What synthetic lubricant is presently used with ternary blends containing HCFCs?
Core.explanation: Ester oils are commonly associated with many blended refrigerants.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: Which process cleans refrigerant for immediate reuse by oil separation and filtering?
Core.explanation: Recycling cleans refrigerant for reuse. Recovering removes it; reclaiming restores it to new specifications.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: Which refrigerant contains chlorine?
Core.explanation: R-22 is an HCFC and contains chlorine.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: Refrigerant cylinders should be all of the following EXCEPT:
Core.explanation: Recovery cylinders should not be filled to 95%; remember the 80% fill safety rule.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: Which action violates the venting prohibition?
Core.explanation: Cutting a refrigerant line before evacuation is intentional venting.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: How should refrigerant blends be charged?
Core.explanation: Blends are charged as liquid to prevent fractionation.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: Which process returns refrigerant to new product specifications and requires chemical analysis?
Core.explanation: Reclaiming restores refrigerant to new-product specifications.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: What factor affects the required evacuation level?
Core.explanation: Evacuation requirements depend on appliance type and size/category.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: Which refrigerant has the lowest GWP among these?
Core.explanation: R-600a has very low GWP compared with common HFCs.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: Which refrigerant type has the highest ODP?
Core.explanation: CFCs have the highest ozone depletion potential.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: HFC refrigerants contain which elements?
Core.explanation: HFC = hydrofluorocarbon: hydrogen, fluorine, and carbon.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: Most effective method for locating a small refrigerant leak:
Core.explanation: Small leaks usually require electronic or ultrasonic detection.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: How should disposable refrigerant cylinders be discarded?
Core.explanation: Recover remaining refrigerant, render the cylinder unusable, then recycle the metal.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: What is being done in the U.S. to reduce ozone damage?
answers: Use natural gas instead of coal
Core.explanation: Policy focuses on phasing out ozone-depleting refrigerants.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: What does Section 608 certification allow?
Core.explanation: 608 allows purchase and handling of regulated stationary HVAC refrigerants.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: What does a compound low-side gauge use below atmospheric pressure?
Core.explanation: Compound gauges show positive pressure in psig and vacuum in inches of mercury.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: Which is NOT a recommended safety practice?
Core.explanation: The random 5-foot rule is not a standard requirement.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: If R-410A was added to an R-22 system, what should be done?
Core.explanation: Mixed refrigerant is contaminated and should be recovered separately.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: What penalty may be imposed for Clean Air Act violations?
Core.explanation: Technicians can lose certification in addition to other penalties.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: State of refrigerant entering the compressor:
Core.explanation: Compressors are designed to compress vapor.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: Baseline gas for GWP:
Core.explanation: CO2 is the reference gas for GWP.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: Purpose of low-loss fittings:
Core.explanation: Low-loss fittings reduce emissions.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: Recovery means:
Core.explanation: Recovery means removing refrigerant and storing it externally.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Core.question: Oil stains near fittings can indicate:
Core.explanation: Leaks often carry oil, so oil stains are a clue.
Core.tip: Focus on the safest, most complete compliant action.

Type I.question: High heat can decompose CFC/HCFC refrigerants into:
Type I.explanation: High temperatures can create dangerous acids.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: A reclamation facility receiving mixed refrigerant may:
Type I.explanation: Mixed refrigerants may be refused or cost more to process.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Recovering from a refrigerator with inoperative compressor into non-pressurized container may require:
answers: Chasing refrigerant from oil with dry nitrogen
Type I.explanation: Dry nitrogen can help move trapped refrigerant from oil.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Large refrigerant leak can cause suffocation because refrigerants:
answers: Are heavier than air and displace oxygen
Type I.explanation: Many refrigerants displace oxygen in low areas.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: If refrigerant in recovery cylinder is suspected contaminated:
Type I.explanation: Contaminated refrigerant should be sent for reclamation.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Passive system-dependent recovery for small appliances:
Type I.explanation: Passive recovery uses pressure/temperature differences.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Direct drop-in substitute for R-22:
answers: There is no true drop-in substitute
Type I.explanation: R-22 substitutes require compatibility checks; true drop-ins are not assumed.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: 80% capture from small appliance with non-operating compressor applies when using:
Type I.explanation: This 80% requirement is tied to system-dependent passive recovery.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Passive recovery permitted for:
Type I.explanation: Passive recovery is associated with small appliances.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Typical service aperture on small appliances:
Type I.explanation: Small appliances often use a process tube accessed with a piercing valve.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Recommended safe practice when recovering:
answers: Ventilation only if oil fog appears
Type I.explanation: Eye protection and proper gloves reduce injury risk.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Type I certification applies to:
answers: 5 lb or less hermetically sealed/factory charged appliances
Type I.explanation: Type I covers small factory-charged appliances.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Maximum factory charge for Type I appliance:
Type I.explanation: Small appliance threshold is 5 pounds or less.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: New household refrigerators may be factory charged with all EXCEPT:
Type I.explanation: R-404A is not the typical household refrigerator factory charge in this context.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: When installing access fitting on sealed system:
Type I.explanation: Access fittings should be leak tested before recovery.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Why install high and low side access valves recovering household refrigerator?
Type I.explanation: Access to both sides improves recovery speed/completeness.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Why let recovery cylinder temperature stabilize before pressure reading?
Type I.explanation: PT readings require stable known conditions.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Maximum normal charge allowed for system-dependent recovery equipment:
Type I.explanation: 15 pounds is the key limit.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Access valves needed with non-operating compressor and restricted capillary tube:
Type I.explanation: Both sides need access if restriction traps refrigerant.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Nitrogen tank used in repair must have:
Type I.explanation: Use nitrogen only with a regulator.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: When R-410A is recovered:
Type I.explanation: Recover R-410A into a clearly marked vessel.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Before beginning recovery, always:
Type I.explanation: Know the refrigerant type to prevent mixing.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Which does NOT meet Type I criteria?
Type I.explanation: MVAC-like systems are treated differently.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Certification required for recovering from small appliances:
Type I.explanation: Small appliance recovery requires Type I or Universal.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type I.question: Key Type I memory rule:
Type I.explanation: Type I = small appliances.
Type I.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: Dry nitrogen used to break vacuum during triple evacuation:
Type II.explanation: Nitrogen cylinders require a regulator.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: Before major repair on >200 lb R-410A appliance, evacuate to:
Type II.explanation: 25 mm Hg absolute is the test value here.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: When is system-dependent recovery against EPA rules?
Type II.explanation: System-dependent recovery is limited by charge size.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: After liquid refrigerant is recovered, remaining vapor is:
Type II.explanation: Remaining vapor must be recovered.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: When could reciprocating compressor be damaged if energized?
Type II.explanation: Closed discharge valve can cause dangerous pressure.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: After isolating compressor in 40 lb R-404A system:
answers: Evacuate isolated section to 0 psig and verify no rise
Type II.explanation: Evacuate isolated section and verify pressure does not rise.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: When can appliance be recovered to atmospheric pressure?
Type II.explanation: If leaks make prescribed recovery unattainable, special limits apply.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: After reaching required recovery vacuum:
answers: Wait to see if pressure rises
Type II.explanation: A pressure rise may indicate remaining refrigerant or leaks.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: Follow-up verification after supermarket rack leak repair must be:
Type II.explanation: Follow-up verification is generally within 10 days.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: At start of recovery, which phase removed to save time?
Type II.explanation: Liquid recovery moves more refrigerant mass faster.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: First look for on leaking hermetic system:
Type II.explanation: Oil traces are a common leak clue.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: Remove moisture from refrigerant in system by:
Type II.explanation: Filter driers remove moisture and contaminants.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: Non-hermetic belt-driven compressor unused for months likely leaks at:
Type II.explanation: Open-drive shaft seals can leak after sitting.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: Vapor recovery minimizes loss of:
Type II.explanation: Vapor recovery reduces oil carryover.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: Speed up refrigerant removal by:
Type II.explanation: Cooling the recovery cylinder lowers pressure.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: Refrigerant leaving receiver is:
Type II.explanation: Receiver outlet is high-pressure liquid.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: Recovering R-410A after R-22 in recovery machine requires:
Type II.explanation: Avoid mixing refrigerants; clear unit and change filter.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: Hermetic recovery machine may overheat in deep vacuum because:
answers: Motor relies on refrigerant flow cooling
Type II.explanation: Hermetic motors need refrigerant flow for cooling.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: Connect hose for liquid recovery at:
Type II.explanation: Liquid recovery is from the liquid line.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: Which repair is always major?
Type II.explanation: Evaporator coil replacement is a major repair.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: Detector not required with continuous ventilation for:
Type II.explanation: R-717/ammonia has different safety group treatment.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: Best charging method for 80 lb system with air-cooled condenser/evaporator:
Type II.explanation: Large systems are charged liquid through liquid line when appropriate.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: False statement about stationary recovery/recycling equipment:
Type II.explanation: Individual equipment purchases are not generally reported to EPA.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: If initial leak verification fails after repair:
answers: Conduct additional repairs within window or mothball
Type II.explanation: Failed verification means more repair action is needed.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type II.question: 2019 leak repair threshold for commercial refrigeration with 50+ lb R-22:
Type II.explanation: Commercial refrigeration threshold is commonly 20%.
Type II.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: ASHRAE 15-2013 room sensors/alarms detect:
Type III.explanation: Machine room detection broadly addresses refrigerant safety.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: When should appliance NOT be evacuated to prescribed level?
answers: Repair followed by evacuation to environment
Type III.explanation: Leaks can make the prescribed level unattainable.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: How long keep records for disposal evacuation >5 and <50 lb?
Type III.explanation: 3 years is the key recordkeeping value.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Low-pressure refrigerant removal starts with:
Type III.explanation: Low-pressure recovery starts with liquid removal when possible.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: ASHRAE 15 room sensors and alarms detect:
Type III.explanation: Sensors detect refrigerant leaks.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: After reaching recovery vacuum on low-pressure appliance:
answers: Wait to see if pressure rises
Type III.explanation: Wait to see if system pressure rises.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: True statement about recovery/recycling equipment:
Type III.explanation: Equipment must be certified and labeled.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Which action is always major?
Type III.explanation: Evaporator coil replacement is major.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Reduce refrigerant loss from R-123 chiller purge unit by:
Type III.explanation: Fix leaks to reduce air infiltration and purge losses.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Best method to determine chiller normal charge:
Type III.explanation: Use the nameplate charge.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Hydrostatic tube test kit will:
Type III.explanation: Hydrostatic testing finds tube leaks.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Max nitrogen test pressure for low-pressure centrifugal:
Type III.explanation: 10 psig is the common test value.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: R-123 at 14.7 PSIA boils at approx:
Type III.explanation: R-123 boils around 82°F at atmospheric pressure.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Typical high-pressure cut-out on recovery unit for low-pressure chiller:
Type III.explanation: 10 psig is a common setting.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: After liquid recovery from low-pressure chiller:
Type III.explanation: Recover remaining vapor.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Primary purpose of purge unit:
Type III.explanation: Purge units remove non-condensables.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Oversized vacuum pump could:
Type III.explanation: Rapid evacuation can freeze trapped water.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Vapor remaining in average 350-ton R-123 chiller after liquid removal at 0 psig:
Type III.explanation: A large amount of vapor can remain.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Who keeps leak inspection and verification records?
Type III.explanation: Owner/operator keeps records.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Pressurize R-123 after non-major repair by:
Type III.explanation: Warming refrigerant raises pressure without adding non-condensables.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Where does refrigerant go after purge unit?
Type III.explanation: It goes to the recovery/service vessel.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Where does purge unit take suction?
Type III.explanation: Non-condensables collect at top of condenser.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Why introduce vapor before liquid after servicing?
answers: Raises pressure slowly, preventing rupture disk failure
Liquid in deep vacuum may freeze water in tubes
Type III.explanation: Liquid in deep vacuum can rapidly evaporate and freeze water.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Comfort cooling chillers with 50+ lb ODS refrigerant repair leaks above:
Type III.explanation: Comfort cooling threshold is commonly 10%.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.

Type III.question: Charged low-pressure machines most efficiently leak checked by:
Type III.explanation: Heating raises pressure and helps find leaks.
Type III.tip: Connect the rule to the equipment type and recovery procedure.
